petitioner_background: The Petitioner is a television host from Ukraine/Russia seeking classification as an individual of extraordinary ability under EB-1A. He has worked as a TV host/presenter on Russian and Ukrainian television channels since 2011, including as co-host of a popular reality television show, and has created and produced his own TV shows. He has approximately 1.9 million Instagram followers and has endorsement deals with international brands. The Director of the Texas Service Center denied the petition, finding he met only one criterion (judging), and on appeal he claimed to meet six additional criteria.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) Telenedelya magazine's prize (2014) based on receiving the most votes in a category, with rules stating voting was via SMS-messages or the site tv.ua/tz, open to any Ukrainian TV viewer; (2) Recognition by Cosmopolitan magazine's Ukrainian edition in 2019, with voting rules allowing one vote per social media account or mailbox, limited to Ukrainian citizens residing in Ukraine, voting from March 1 to May 21, 2019 on cosmo.com.ua; (3) Promotional information about his awards from the magazines' websites and some media coverage related to winner announcements; (4) Five awards won by the reality television show on which he was co-host, including Russian Academy of Television awards in 2014, 2016 and 2018, and National Council of Television and Radio Broadcasting of Ukraine awards in 2015 and 2016.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the documentation did not indicate the significance of his Telenedelya and Cosmopolitan awards in the field of endeavor. The record did not include evidence demonstrating that the awards were recognized by the field in general rather than mainly limited to the readership of Telenedelya or the Ukrainian edition of Cosmopolitan. The limited media coverage did not rise to the level of national or international recognition. Regarding the five show awards, the AAO determined that to satisfy this criterion the Petitioner must demonstrate he was the recipient of prizes or awards, and the documentation announcing the winners did not identify the Petitioner as their recipient, citing Hristov v. Roark, 2011 WL 4711885 (E.D.N.Y. 2011) (finding that a producer and star actor was not the recipient of awards for Best Short Film and Best Cinematography since he was not named on either award).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted articles about him in Cosmopolitan (Ukrainian edition), National Geographic (Russian edition), Otdokhni!, Hello! (Russian edition), GQ (Russian edition), Mediananny.com, Telenedelya, www.gazeta.ru, https://istochnik.ru, www.footboom.ru, www.stb.ua, and ua.tribuna.com. He also submitted screenshots from similarweb.com regarding website traffic, including for Cosmopolitan (Ukrainian edition) showing Total Visits of 660.25K, Global Rank 106,481, Country Rank 2,126, Category Rank 339. In response to the RFE, he provided two additional articles about him in L'Officiel (2021) and Privat Space (2020).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the authors of articles in Cosmopolitan, National Geographic, Otdokhni!, Mediananny.com, Telenedelya, www.footboom.ru, and www.stb.ua were not identified as required by the criterion. The Petitioner did not establish the significance of the similarweb.com statistics, showing that the magazines and websites represent major trade publications or other major media (per USCIS Policy Manual requiring establishment that circulation is high compared to other circulation statistics). The two additional articles in L'Officiel (2021) and Privat Space (2020) were published after the petition was filed, and eligibility must be demonstrated at the time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), (12).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Not discussed in detail on appeal; the Director determined the Petitioner fulfilled this criterion of participation as a judge of others' work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that the Petitioner fulfilled this criterion at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv), and this was not contested on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) Letters from a general manager at a Russian channel (dated 2020 and 2021) stating the Petitioner worked as TV host/presenter of their most popular show since 2013, became the pivotal part of programming, and that the channel won numerous awards with him as host and 'creative visionary' behind the show's success, and that he created and produced his own TV shows (2014, 2016-2019, and others) that became very popular in Russia and CIS; (2) Letter from a director of a production studio stating the show consistently received very high ratings and achieved tremendous success in Ukraine, CIS, and Israel, with all seasons being hugely popular and viewed by millions; (3) Letter from a producer of another show stating the Petitioner worked as host for three full seasons starting in 2016, joining the original host for six years, and the show enjoyed a great spike in popularity and ratings during his tenure; (4) Letter from an administrative director describing him as extraordinarily talented and as a huge social media influencer with almost 2 million Instagram followers; (5) Screenshot of Instagram profile showing 1.9 million followers; (6) Promotional information from a channel website; (7) YouTube screenshot and nine feedback comments posted at otzyvru.com; (8) YouTube screenshots from 2021 showing subscriber increase from 166,000 (May 2021) to 217,000 (November 2021); (9) Argument about contracts with international brands.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the letters did not offer sufficiently detailed information, nor did the record include adequate corroborating documentation to demonstrate the nature of specific 'original' contributions that have been considered of major significance. While awards recognized the show's popularity and creativity, the evidence did not demonstrate that the Petitioner's original work has influenced the entertainment industry to the extent it is of major significance. The record lacked published television rankings or other statistical evidence to corroborate claims about viewership in the millions. The AAO noted that letters lacking specifics and using hyperbolic language are not probative, citing USCIS Policy Manual and 1756, Inc. v. The U.S. Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9, 15 (D.C. Dist. 1990) (USCIS need not accept primarily conclusory statements). The YouTube subscription growth post-dated the filing of the petition, and eligibility must be demonstrated at time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1). The 1.9 million Instagram followers showed popularity but did not represent an original contribution of major significance. Neither endorsement deals nor popularity in the television industry demonstrated that his original work rose to the level of artistic or business-related contributions of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner claimed leading or critical roles for: (1) A production company, with two letters from the company's director stating he worked as 'leading co-host' of a show from August 2011 to November 2013, 2015, and 2019 to March 2020, was the second male co-host in the show's history, improvised memorable moments, and was an integral part of the team's 'brain' during brainstorming; (2) A Russian channel, with letters from the general manager asserting he was the 'creative visionary' behind the show's lasting success, set the tone for the show's success, used one-of-a-kind artistic talent, had a 'leftover effect' after leaving temporarily, contributed significantly to increasing brand recognition and profits, and created/produced his own TV shows (2014, 2016-2019); (3) A Ukrainian city, with October 2020 and April 2021 letters from the mayor explaining he was invited to be a representative of youth and liaison between residents and municipal authorities, conducted regular meetings with the city's youth, and filmed a video called showing city sites including the coast, attracting tourists from Ukraine and foreign countries; (4) Ukrainian Soccer Club, with a letter (author not identified) stating he supported the team by attending matches, visiting training camp, bonding with players, mentioning the club during an episode, participating in charity auctions, and sending video messages; (5) A brand, with a letter from the head of marketing stating he became the face of the brand in Ukraine and celebrity brand ambassador starting winter 2020, starring in commercials for winter 2020, spring 2021 and summer 2021 collections; (6) A television show, with a letter from the show's producer discussing his work as host.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Regarding the production company, the director did not provide specific, detailed information demonstrating the nature of the Petitioner's leading or critical role, and the record lacked evidence showing the company has a distinguished reputation. Regarding the Russian channel, the AAO found the record did not include television ratings data or company financial statements to corroborate claims about brand recognition and profits, the Petitioner had not shown that his role as co-host or producer constitutes a leading or critical role for the channel, and no evidence showed the company has a distinguished reputation. Regarding the Ukrainian city, the mayor did not provide specific data or corroborating evidence about tourism effects, the statements were not sufficient to demonstrate the role was leading or critical (e.g., no showing of leadership position or responsibility for successes or outcomes of operations or financial standing), and no evidence indicated the city has a distinguished reputation. Regarding the Ukrainian Soccer Club, the Petitioner did not demonstrate how the described roles showed a leading position or resulted in critical or essential outcomes for the team. Regarding the brand, the role post-dated the filing of the petition per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), (12). Regarding the television show, the AAO cited Merriam-Webster definitions of 'organization' and 'establishment' and determined that a television show does not constitute an organization or establishment as required by 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii), and the Petitioner had not demonstrated the show has a distinguished reputation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) April 2021 letter from a production studio director stating her company owes the tremendous commercial success of their TV production to the Petitioner's contributions, and thanks to his original talent and expertise the show became one of the top shows in Ukraine and Russia of all times based on viewership and brand recognition; (2) Separate statement from the same letter-writer asserting her company paid the Petitioner $11,000.00 per episode for 19 episodes (from #412 to #430); (3) YouTube screenshots for various 2011-2013 episodes (seasons two through seven) showing 'views' ranging from 334,957 to 2,391,647.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the record did not include independent television ratings reports, objective viewership data for programming, or other evidence to corroborate the director's claim. The compensation report reflected the Petitioner's remuneration for filming episodes rather than the commercial success of his work as shown by 'sales' or 'receipts.' The Petitioner had not demonstrated that YouTube 'views' translate to his commercial successes relative to others in the industry in the form of 'sales' or 'receipts.' The evidence was insufficient to establish that the volume of sales or television revenue reflects the Petitioner's commercial successes relative to others involved in similar pursuits in the performing arts.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the Petitioner failed to satisfy at least three of the ten regulatory criteria under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). He met only the judging criterion as determined by the Director, and failed to establish awards, published material, original contributions, leading or critical role, or commercial successes. The AAO declined to reach the high salary criterion claimed on appeal because the Petitioner could not fulfill the initial three-criterion requirement, and did not need to provide a final merits determination under Kazarian. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded that it did not support a finding of the required acclaim and recognition, noting this is a highly restrictive visa classification for those already at the top of their fields rather than progressing toward it, and the Petitioner had not shown sustained national or international acclaim consistent with a 'career of acclaimed work.'
legal_citations: Immigration and Nationality Act section 203(b)(1)(A), 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Strategati, LLC v. Sessions, 2019 WL 2330181 (S.D. CA 2019)
1756, Inc. v. The U.S. Att'y Gen., 745 F. Supp. 9 (D.C. Dist. 1990)
Matter of Price, 20 I&N Dec. 953 (Assoc. Comm'r 1994)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The letter regarding the Ukrainian Soccer Club was signed but the author's identity was not identified in the record, which the AAO noted as a deficiency. Several pieces of evidence (L'Officiel and Privat Space articles, YouTube subscription growth, and the brand ambassadorship role) post-dated the petition filing and were excluded under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1), (12), which requires eligibility at the time of filing.
